question.text: The risk factors disclosed in the SID include:

answer.text: Both standard risk factors common to all mutual fund schemes and scheme-specific risk factors
Only scheme-specific risk factors unique to that particular scheme
Only standard risk factors that apply uniformly to all mutual fund schemes
Risk factors are not disclosed in the SID — they are part of the SAI

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** The SID must disclose both: (1) Standard risk factors that are common to all mutual fund investments (market risk, liquidity risk, etc.), and (2) Scheme-specific risk factors that are unique to the particular scheme based on its investment strategy, asset class, and structure.
**The Trap:** The SID covers BOTH types of risk factors — not just one. Risk factors are a key component of the SID, not the SAI. Candidates who answer hastily may pick "only scheme-specific" thinking standard risks are too obvious to mention.
**Cross-Exam Context:** In NISM V-B and XII, risk disclosure is a major compliance area. Understanding that both standard and specific risks must be disclosed helps with questions about regulatory requirements and investor protection.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When explaining risks to clients, start with standard risks (market, credit, liquidity) to set the baseline, then layer in scheme-specific risks. A client investing in a credit risk fund needs to understand both general market risk AND the specific concentration risk of lower-rated bonds.
